e: 다하다 (da-ha-da) describes being completely worn out or depleted. Often used in contexts of physical or mental fatigue.
연이어 (yeon-i-eo) means occurring one after another without interruption. Often describes events, days, or actions in sequence.
필수 (pil-su) describes something absolutely necessary or essential. Cannot be omitted or replaced.
모의 (mo-ui) refers to a fake version or copy of something. Often used for mock exams, imitation products, or simulations.
연상 (yeon-sang) means to think of or recall something related. Describes the mental act of connecting ideas.
적정 (jeok-jeong) means appropriate, fitting, or at the right level. Denotes proper balance or correctness.
반기다 (ban-gi-da) means to greet warmly or welcome someone. Expresses hospitality and friendly reception.
걸다 (geol-da) means to hang, place, or set something up. Used for installing, hanging pictures, or setting traps.
상관 (sang-gwan) can mean both a superior (boss) or a relationship/correlation. Context determines the meaning.
항 (hang) refers to a section, clause, or paragraph—a division of text or legal document. Comes from Sino-Korean roots.
진작 (jin-jak) is an adverb meaning 'long ago' or 'early on.' Expresses time far in the past or regret about timing.
병행 (byeong-haeng) means running alongside or in parallel. Describes simultaneous actions or events that progress together.
영상 (yeong-sang) means an image, picture, or reflection. Often used for video content, photos, or visual reflections.
긴장 (gin-jang) means tension, stress, or strain. Can refer to physical tension or emotional stress.
자고 (ja-go) is the conjunction form of 자다 (sleep). Used when describing sleeping as one action before another.
일반인 (il-ban-in) refers to a regular person, the general public, or someone without special status or expertise.
며 (myeo) is a particle used to list items or actions together, meaning 'and' in connective contexts.

s: 처세 means 'art of living' in Korean
e: 처세 (cheo-se) refers to the art or way of living well in the world—worldly wisdom and the secret to getting along.
수험표 (su-heom-pyo) is the official identification slip given to exam takers. Essential for checking into test sites.
라고 (ra-go) is a particle used to quote or indicate how something is called or named. Essential for reported speech.

s: 관하다 means 'be connected with' in Korean
e: 관하다 (gwan-ha-da) means to be concerned with or related to something. Indicates a connection or relevance.
작성 (jak-seong) means to compose, write, or draw up a document. Used for creating forms, reports, or contracts.
보편성 (bo-pyeon-seong) refers to the quality of being universal or applicable to all. The state of being widespread.
초유 (cho-yu) refers to something unprecedented or happening for the very first time. Often used for record-breaking events.
핑 (ping) is an onomatopoeia describing being stunned or dazed by surprise. Often used casually for that blank moment.
낮추다 (nat-chu-da) means to lower, reduce, or make something go down. Can be physical or metaphorical.
뚫리다 (ttul-li-da) means to be pierced, broken through, or opened. Describes something that's been penetrated.
정규 (jeong-gyu) means regular, official, or formal. Used for standard procedures and full-time positions.

s: 남녀 means 'man and woman' in Korean
e: 남녀 (nam-nyeo) is the compound of 남 (man) and 녀 (woman), referring to both genders together.

s: 내외 means 'inside and outside' in Korean
e: 내외 (nae-oe) means interior and exterior, or about/approximately. Also used in names to indicate a range.
다량 (da-ryang) means a large quantity or abundance. Often used in scientific or formal contexts.
그만큼 (geu-man-kkeum) means 'to that degree' or 'that much.' Refers to a quantity or degree just mentioned.
기리다 (gi-ri-da) means to praise, admire, or applaud. Often used for honoring achievements.
혼잣말 (hon-ja-mal) means talking to oneself or a soliloquy. When someone speaks but only they hear it.
무인 (mu-in) means unmanned or without people. Used for automatic systems, unmanned shops, or empty places.
눈빛 (nun-bbit) literally means the light/color of the eyes. Describes the appearance or expression in someone's gaze.
점도 (jeom-do) is a physics term for how thick or sticky a liquid is. Higher viscosity means more resistance to flow.
그루 (geu-ru) refers to a tree stump or the base left after a tree has been cut. Also used as a unit for counting trees.
탓 (tat) means fault, blame, cause, or the consequences of an action. Often used in the phrase '탓이다' (it's the fault of).
잠시 (jam-si) means a short time, a moment, or briefly. Often used to ask for temporary patience.
확 (hwak) is an onomatopoeia adverb meaning suddenly or abruptly. Expresses quick, dramatic action.
깃들이다 (git-deul-i-da) means to settle, lodge, or dwell in a place. Often used poetically.
거치다 (geo-chi-da) means to pass by, drop in on, or pass through a place briefly.
구분 (gu-bun) means to divide, classify, or make a distinction between things.
덕분 (deok-bun) means thanks to, or the benefit received. Often expresses gratitude for someone's help.
보람 (bo-ram) means worth, value, or the satisfaction of accomplishment. Often used as '보람있다' (worthwhile).
자발적 (ja-bal-jeok) means done willingly and from one's own choice. Opposite of forced or mandatory.
참모습 (cham-mo-seup) means the real form or true nature of something, beneath any disguise.
년도 (nyeon-do) is a formal/written version of 해 (year). Used in official contexts like documents and formal speech.
짚다 (jjip-da) means to lean on, support, or rest against something for stability.
이면 refers to the reverse or back side of something, commonly used for physical objects or arguments.
신경 refers to nerves in the body or can mean caring/concern about something in daily speech.

s: 오히려 means 'on the contrary' in Korean
e: 오히려 is a conjunction expressing contrast or opposite meaning, often used to correct a previous statement.
달리 is an adverb meaning in a different way or manner, emphasizing distinction from the norm.
꼽다 means to count or number things, often used when enumerating items or selecting from a group.
혹시 expresses possibility or uncertainty, often used when asking cautious or tentative questions.
금연 is a compound word: 금 (forbid) + 연 (smoke), commonly seen on signs and in buildings.
안내 means guidance or information service, used for tourist guides, customer service announcements, or directional help.
멋 originally refers to taste or zest in eating, but modernly means style or coolness in casual speech.
저래 is a colloquial contraction of '저렇게' meaning in that manner or like that, commonly used in casual speech.
그나마 expresses acceptance of something despite it being imperfect or limited — 'at least that much' or 'still'.

s: 골탕 means 'suffer a big loss' in Korean
e: 골탕 describes falling for a prank or suffering a bitter experience, often in a 골탕을 먹다 (eat a bone broth) expression.
최저 is a formal adjective meaning the minimum or lowest point, commonly used in prices, temperatures, and ratings.
부쩍 is an adverb describing stubborn or persistent behavior, done with a fixed resolve or reluctance.
각종 means various kinds or all sorts, used as a prefix to describe multiple types of something.
밥맛 literally means 'rice taste' but idiomatically refers to one's appetite or mood about eating.
연속 means succession, continuity, or unbroken sequence — the state of things continuing without interruption.
량 is a formal or written noun meaning quantity or amount, more commonly written as 양 in modern Korean.
때문 is the noun meaning reason or cause, used in '~때문에' construction to show causality.
각각 means individually or separately, emphasizing that something applies to every single item in a group.
외 is a postposition meaning 'other than' or 'except for', used in the structure '~외에(는)'.
어찌나 is an exclamatory adverb expressing surprise or excess, emphasizing intensity of a quality or quantity.
회색 is the color between black and white, commonly used to describe neutral or sad moods.
공공 means public or communal, used as an adjective prefix for things belonging to or benefiting everyone.
어떡하다 is an exclamatory expression of confusion or distress, a contraction of '어떻게 하다'.
불러들이다 means to call someone to come inside or to summon them, emphasizing the inward direction.
온라인 is a loanword from English meaning connected to the internet or happening in cyberspace.
들이다 means to put in or invest, used for both literal and figurative meanings like investing money or effort.
단절 means cutting off or discontinuation, often used for breaking relationships or interrupting communication.
신규 refers to something new or recently established, commonly used for new accounts, regulations, or employment.
빼다 means to take out or remove something, with uses ranging from literal (taking out an object) to mathematical (subtraction).

s: 넘기다 means 'pass (a thing) over' in Korean
e: 넘기다 means to transfer, hand over, or move something to another person or place.
맞춤 means custom-made or made-to-order, indicating something prepared specifically per request.
미만 means less than or under a certain amount, used in comparisons and age restrictions.
두각 means to show distinctive ability or prominence, used in '두각을 나타내다' (display outstanding ability).
별것 means something rare or unusual, a curious or peculiar object — often used in negative constructions.
분양 means to divide and distribute, especially in real estate when land or apartments are sold in portions.
자동 means automatic or self-operated, used for machines and processes that run without manual intervention.
바가지 literally means a large bowl, but idiomatically means to be cheated on price — '바가지를 쓰다' (wear a bowl).

s: 를 means 'grammar particle for subject to verb' in Korean
a: grammar particle for subject to verb
e: 를 is an object particle used after a word ending in a vowel, marking the direct object of a verb.
그토록 is an adverb emphasizing degree or extent, meaning to such an extent or degree.
대하다 means to face or confront someone/something, or formally to address/treat a situation.
안부 means well-being or safety, commonly heard in the phrase '안부 인사' (greeting asking about someone's welfare).

s: 이어 means 'connecting part of a sentence' in Korean
e: 이어 is a connector or conjunction that links sentences or clauses together, showing continuity.
절대 means absolute, unconditional, or never, used as adjective or adverb for strong emphasis.

s: 긋다 means 'draw a line' in Korean
e: 긋다 means to draw or strike a line with a tool like a ruler or match, creating a clear mark.
구석구석 means every nook and cranny or every corner, emphasizing thorough coverage or exploration.
접하다 means to come into contact with or touch something, including meeting something for the first time.
각자 means each person individually, emphasizing that everyone has their own separate responsibility or characteristic.
자라나다 means to grow up or develop, especially used for children growing physically and mentally.
